ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for burrata with grilled vegetables. Mapped to the closest defensible FoodOn class 'cheese food product' (FOODON_03315873). A more specific composite dish class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mozzarella and similar fresh cheeses — used as closest FoodEx2 analogue for burrata (a fresh stretched-curd cheese in the mozzarella family); composite dish context noted.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated burrata composite dish code. A00LC (mozzarella and similar fresh cheeses) is the closest defensible anchor. Grilled vegetable and pesto components are not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for burrata or this composite Italian cheese-and-vegetable dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One appetizer plate of approximately 300 g, comprising a whole burrata cheese ball (~125 g) surrounded by an assortment of grilled seasonal vegetables and drizzled with basil pesto sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: mozzarella (fresh), grilled mixed vegetables, basil pesto, olive oil
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA SR Legacy entries for fresh mozzarella/burrata (FDC ID 170845), grilled mixed vegetables (zucchini, eggplant, bell pepper, mushroom), extra virgin olive oil (FDC ID 171413), and basil pesto (FDC ID 172237). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this component estimate and retained where consistent. Estimated dish composition: ~42% burrata, ~40% grilled vegetables, ~10% pesto sauce, ~8% olive oil drizzle.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using mixed non-starchy grilled vegetables and fresh cheese composite.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for burrata with grilled vegetables and pesto. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) fresh mozzarella/burrata has negligible carbohydrate and does not meaningfully contribute to GI; (2) grilled non-starchy vegetables (eggplant, zucchini, bell pepper, mushroom) have GI values of 15–25 per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) basil pesto contributes minimal carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~35. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 5.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 3) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 216.7 mg/100 g; saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g from burrata
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 216.7 mg/100 g is moderate; a full 300 g serving delivers ~650 mg sodium, representing approximately 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, so patients with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced pesto application to lower the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 5.0 g/100 g; low GI ~35; high fat moderates glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 5.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 35, this dish produces a low glycemic load (~5.3 per serving) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management, though the calorie density from fat (12.7 g/100 g) warrants portion awareness for weight-conscious patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g from burrata; partially offset by MUFA from extra virgin olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g (18 g per 300 g serving) exceeds the AHA single-meal guidance of ~7 g saturated fat; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to half a plate and note that the extra virgin olive oil component provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, bell pepper); saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or substitute nightshade vegetables (eggplant, bell pepper) which may exacerbate joint inflammation, and should limit portion due to saturated fat exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold, though oleocanthal from extra virgin olive oil and beta-glucans from oyster mushrooms provide counterbalancing anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burrata with Grilled Vegetables and Basil Pesto Sauce (Italian: Burrata Verdure Grigliate Salsa Basilico) is a vegetarian, gluten-free Italian appetizer dish served in Singapore. It features a whole ball of Burrata D.O.P. — a fresh stretched-curd cheese from Puglia, Italy, with a creamy stracciatella and cream filling encased in a thin mozzarella shell — placed on a bed of grilled seasonal vegetables including oyster mushrooms, eggplant, zucchini, and bell peppers, all finished with a vibrant basil pesto sauce and organic extra virgin olive oil. The dish is served cold or at room temperature. The texture contrast between the silky, rich burrata interior and the smoky, slightly charred vegetables is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish provides 160 kcal per 100 g, with 12.7 g fat (predominantly from burrata and olive oil), 6.7 g protein, and only 5.0 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycemic, high-fat, moderate-protein appetizer. Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g is the primary clinical concern for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Sodium at 216.7 mg/100 g is moderate. The dish is rich in calcium (100 mg/100 g) from burrata and provides vitamin C (8.3 mg/100 g) from the grilled vegetables and pesto. The extra virgin olive oil contributes oleocanthal, a natural anti-inflammatory compound. A standard appetizer serving is approximately 300 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 5.3 — classified as low. This dish is well-suited for low-carbohydrate and Mediterranean-style dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is burrata with grilled vegetables healthy?
Is this dish suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in burrata with grilled vegetables?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fresh mozzarella/burrata, grilled mixed vegetables, extra virgin olive oil, and basil pesto. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and retained where consistent with the component estimate. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated by weighted-component method from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Input ai_generated values cross-checked and retained where consistent. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite grilled-vegetable and burrata dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level data exists but dish-level aggregation for these fields was not performed with sufficient confidence to report a single value. 3. potassium_mg reported as 250.0 mg/100 g — estimated from grilled vegetable and mushroom component contributions per USDA SR Legacy; treated as approximate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g) estimated from olive oil and burrata fatty acid profiles per USDA FoodData Central. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ethnic tags 'Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish' from input record are not applicable to this dish and have been removed. 7. This dish is served in Singapore by a GrabFood-listed restaurant; country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the serving context rather than the culinary origin (Italy). 8. Allergens: milk (burrata), tree nuts (pine nuts in pesto). Halal status unconfirmed — burrata sourcing and pesto ingredients require restaurant-level verification. 9. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 mandatory language requirements for a dish served in Singapore. These are descriptive translations of the canonical English name.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON from input. Identified that input ethnic_tags ('Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish') were inapplicable and removed. Corrected food_name to canonical English. Added full ontology with gap notes. Built component-estimated nutrient profile cross-checked against input values. Added GI analogous estimate with full documentation. Built health_context with all three required conditions plus RA from input. Populated LLM training section. Documented all -1 fields in notes.

Iteration 2: Self-scored draft. Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent'. Validator feedback confirmed G6 and Q1/Q8 failures due to missing mandatory local-language names for Chinese, Hindi, Malay, and Tamil.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names to local_names for chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, and hindi. Added all five local-language name strings to the flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language under their respective language keys. Verified aliases consistency: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array. G6 hard gate now satisfied. Q1 and Q8 now pass.
